critic_raw:
This design compares CNN sensitivity for early-stage lung cancer detection against radiologist consensus as a baseline.

The most fundamental flaw is circular evaluation. The gold-standard labels are defined by radiologist consensus (with histopathology for positives and 24-month follow-up for negatives). The CNN is trained to predict these consensus labels. The baseline is then the consensus sensitivity of those same radiologists on the same test set. This is not an independent comparison: the CNN is trained to replicate a signal (consensus label) that is produced by the baseline's own judgment. Any CNN that achieves high accuracy at predicting the consensus labels will, by construction, show performance close to or correlated with the radiologists' sensitivity — it is recovering the decision boundary that the radiologists used. When the design claims the CNN 'outperforms' radiologists, this means the CNN agrees with consensus more often than individual radiologists agree with consensus, not that it has identified cancer cases that the radiologists missed. The experiment conflates replication accuracy with independent detection superiority.

The threshold selection procedure is specified as: 'We select the CNN's decision threshold on the validation set to match or exceed the radiologist consensus sensitivity.' This explicitly engineers the CNN to achieve at least the same sensitivity as the radiologists — the threshold is not freely chosen, it is chosen to produce a comparable sensitivity. The comparison is then between the CNN at a sensitivity-matched threshold and the radiologists at their natural operating point. This is a threshold manipulation that guarantees the CNN cannot lose on sensitivity by construction, not a genuine comparison.

The split is stratified by institution and disease prevalence using a non-temporal (random) stratification. CT scans in hospital archives span 5 years; a temporal split matters because imaging protocols, scanner technology, and radiologist training evolve over time. Images from 2019 and 2024 may have different noise characteristics, reconstruction algorithms, and spatial resolution. A random split means both train and test contain scans from all years, allowing the model to learn scanner-generation-specific features from 2019 data that also appear in 2024 test images. This inflates apparent generalization.
all_issues_raised: The CNN is trained to predict radiologist consensus labels, then compared against the sensitivity of those same radiologists — this is circular evaluation that measures how well the CNN replicates the baseline rather than whether it independently detects cancer.

The CNN's decision threshold is selected on the validation set to match or exceed radiologist sensitivity, which ensures the CNN cannot lose on the primary metric by construction — this is threshold engineering, not genuine performance comparison.

A random stratified split across a 5-year archive means train and test images come from all scanner generations and protocol versions; temporal shift in imaging technology is not isolated, inflating apparent model generalization.
all_issues_adjudicated: The CNN is trained to predict radiologist consensus labels, then compared against the sensitivity of those same radiologists — this is circular evaluation that measures how well the CNN replicates the baseline rather than whether it independently detects cancer.

The CNN's decision threshold is selected on the validation set to match or exceed radiologist sensitivity, which ensures the CNN cannot lose on the primary metric by construction — this is threshold engineering, not genuine performance comparison.

A random stratified split across a 5-year archive means train and test images come from all scanner generations and protocol versions; temporal shift in imaging technology is not isolated, inflating apparent model generalization.
